title: Invalid Sudoku Validator using Backtracking with Diagonal Conflicts Overcounting

statement.markdown: Write a function to validate whether a given 9x9 Sudoku board is a valid solution or not. The board is filled with digits from 1 to 9, representing the different squares. The problem is that the board has some missing digits. By the rules of Sudoku, each row, each column, and each of the nine 3x3 sub-grids must contain all the numbers from 1 to 9 without repetition. The input board is represented as a 2D array of 9x9, where the missing number is represented by a zero. If the board is a valid solution, the function should return True; otherwise, it returns False. The input board is guaranteed to be valid except for some missing numbers.
statement.outputFormat: A boolean value indicating whether the board is a valid solution or not
statement.notes: The function should check each row, column, and 3x3 sub-grid to see if they contain all the numbers from 1 to 9.

constraints.description: A 2D array representing the Sudoku board

hints.text: Check for rows and columns first

editorial.title: Invalid Sudoku Validator using Backtracking with Diagonal Conflicts Overcounting
editorial.intuition: The function should use backtracking to check for each row, column, and 3x3 sub-grid to see if they contain all the numbers from 1 to 9.
editorial.bruteForce: A brute force approach would be to check each row, column, and 3x3 sub-grid for each number from 1 to 9.
editorial.betterSolution: A better approach would be to use backtracking to check all three conditions at once.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution would be to use backtracking to check each number in the 3x3 sub-grids first, then rows and columns.

starterCode.java: public class Solution {public boolean isValid(int[][] board);}
starterCode.python: class Solution: def isValid(self, board: List[List[int]]) -> bool:
